title: Marmion
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heap on more wood! the wind is chill;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But let it whistle as it will,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weן¿½ll keep our Christmas merry still.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Each age has deemן¿½d the new-born year
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The fittest time for festal cheer:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Even, heathen yet, the savage Dane
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At Iol more deep the mead did drain;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ High on the beach his galleys drew,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And feasted all his pirate crew;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then in his low and pine-built hall
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where shields and axes deckן¿½d the wall
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They gorged upon the half-dressן¿½d steer;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Caroused in seas of sable beer;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While round, in brutal jest, were thrown
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The half-gnawן¿½d rib, and marrow-bone:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or listenן¿½d all, in grim delight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While Scalds yellן¿½d out the joys of fight.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then forth, in frenzy, would they hie,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While wildly loose their red locks fly,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And dancing round the blazing pile,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They make such barbarous mirth the while,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As best might to the mind recall
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The boisterous joys of Odinן¿½s hall.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And well our Christian sires of old
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Loved when the year its course had rollן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And brought blithe Christmas back again,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With all his hospitable train.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Domestic and religious rite
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Gave honour to the holy night;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On Christmas Eve the bells were rung;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On Christmas Eve the mass was sung:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That only night in all the year,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Saw the stoled priest the chalice rear.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The damsel donnן¿½d her kirtle sheen;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The hall was dressן¿½d with holly green;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Forth to the wood did merry-men go,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To gather in the mistletoe.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then openן¿½d wide the Baronן¿½s hall
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To vassal, tenant, serf and all;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Power laid his rod of rule aside
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Ceremony doffן¿½d his pride.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The heir, with roses in his shoes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That night might village partner choose;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Lord, underogating, share
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The vulgar game of ן¿½post and pairן¿½.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All hailן¿½d, with uncontrollן¿½d delight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And general voice, the happy night,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That to the cottage, as the crown,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Brought tidings of salvation down.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The fire, with well-dried logs supplied,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Went roaring up the chimney wide;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The huge hall-tableן¿½s oaken face,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Scrubbן¿½d till it shone, the day to grace,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bore then upon its massive board
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No mark to part the squire and lord.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then was brought in the lusty brawn,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By old blue-coated serving-man;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then the grim boarן¿½s head frownן¿½d on high,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Crested with bays and rosemary.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well can the green-garbן¿½d ranger tell,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How, when, and where, the monster fell;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What dogs before his death to tore,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all the baiting of the boar.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The wassel round, in good brown bowls,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Garnishן¿½d with ribbons, blithely trowls.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There the huge sirloin reekן¿½d; hard by
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Plum-porridge stood, and Christmas pie;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor failן¿½d old Scotland to produce,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At such high tide, her savoury goose.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then came the merry makers in,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And carols roarן¿½d with blithesome din;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If unmelodious was the song,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It was a hearty note, and strong.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who lists may in their mumming see
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Traces of ancient mystery;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ White shirts supplied the masquerade,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And smutted cheeks the visors made;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, O! what maskers, richly dight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Can boast of bosoms half so light!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ England was merry England, when
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Old Christmas brought his sports again.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas Christmas broachן¿½d the mightiest ale;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas Christmas told the merriest tale;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A Christmas gambol oft could cheer
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The poor manן¿½s heart through half the year.
meaning:
Walter Scott (Sir)'s contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "Marmion"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
